questions.q: If $|A| = 7$, $|B| = 9$, and $|A \cap B| = 4$, what is $|A \cup B|$?
Let $f\colon \{1,2,3,4\} \to \{a,b,c\}$ be given by $1 \mapsto a,\ 2 \mapsto b,\ 3 \mapsto a,\ 4 \mapsto c$. Which is true?
choices: injective but not surjective
surjective but not injective
bijective
neither injective nor surjective
questions.hint: The image is $\{a,b,c\}$, the whole codomain, so $f$ is surjective.
questions.explain: The image is $\{a,b,c\}$, the whole codomain, so $f$ is surjective. But $f(1) = f(3) = a$ shows $f$ is not injective. With $|A|=4 > 3 = |B|$ no function $A \to B$ can be injective (pigeonhole).

questions.q: How many subsets does a set of size $6$ have? (That is, compute $|\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for $|A| = 6$.)
questions.hint: Each of the $6$ elements is independently in or out of a subset, so $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^6 = 64$.
questions.explain: Each of the $6$ elements is independently in or out of a subset, so $|\mathcal{P}(A)| = 2^6 = 64$.

hard.q: Let $f\colon A \to B$ and $g\colon B \to C$. Which is the correct chain of implications among injectivity and surjectivity of compositions?
choices: $g\circ f$ injective $\Rightarrow$ $f$ injective; $g\circ f$ surjective $\Rightarrow$ $g$ surjective
$g\circ f$ injective $\Rightarrow$ $g$ injective; $g\circ f$ surjective $\Rightarrow$ $f$ surjective
$g\circ f$ injective $\Rightarrow$ both $f$ and $g$ injective
$g\circ f$ surjective $\Rightarrow$ both $f$ and $g$ surjective
hard.explain: If $g\circ f$ is injective and $f(a)=f(a')$, then $g(f(a))=g(f(a'))$, so $a=a'$; thus $f$ is injective. Conversely $g$ need not be (e.g. $g$ can collapse elements outside $\operatorname{im} f$). If $g\circ f$ is surjective then every $c\in C$ is hit, so $g$ is surjective; $f$ need not be.

hard.q: Sets $A$, $B$, $C$ satisfy $|A|=10$, $|B|=8$, $|C|=6$, $|A\cap B|=3$, $|A\cap C|=2$, $|B\cap C|=4$, $|A\cap B\cap C|=1$. Compute $|A\cup B\cup C|$.
Which is a correct counterexample showing that $f^{-1}(f(S)) = S$ can fail?
choices: Take $f=\mathrm{id}$ and $S=\emptyset$; then $f^{-1}(f(\emptyset))=\emptyset\ne S$.
Take $f$ surjective and $S=A$; then $f^{-1}(f(A))\supsetneq A$.
The equality always holds for injective $f$ and can never fail.
hard.explain: $f^{-1}(f(S))\supseteq S$ always, with equality iff $f$ is injective on the relevant domain. A non-injective $f$ can collapse elements outside $S$ into $f(S)$, enlarging the preimage. The example above is the minimal witness.

questions.q: If $|A| = 5$ and $|B| = 4$, how many functions are there from $A$ to $B$? (That is, compute $|B^A|$.)
questions.hint: Each of the $|A|=5$ elements picks a value in $B$ independently, so $|B^A| = |B|^{|A|} = 4^5 = 1024$.
questions.explain: Each of the $|A|=5$ elements picks a value in $B$ independently, so $|B^A| = |B|^{|A|} = 4^5 = 1024$.

questions.q: Let $A$ be a set. Under which bijection is $\mathcal{P}(A) \cong \{0,1\}^A$?
choices: $S \mapsto $ its characteristic polynomial
$S \mapsto $ the indicator function $\mathbf{1}_S$, with $\mathbf{1}_S(a)=1$ iff $a\in S$
$S \mapsto |S|$
$S \mapsto A \setminus S$ (which is not a bijection to $\{0,1\}^A$)
questions.hint: A subset $S\subseteq A$ is determined by, and determines, its indicator function $\mathbf{1}_S\colon A\to\{0,1\}$.
questions.explain: A subset $S\subseteq A$ is determined by, and determines, its indicator function $\mathbf{1}_S\colon A\to\{0,1\}$. This bijection is also why $|\mathcal{P}(A)|=2^{|A|}$.

questions.q: Let $|A|=3$ and $|B|=2$. How many elements are in $\mathcal{P}(A\times B)$?

hard.q: Compute $|\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}(\{a,b\}))|$, i.e. the size of the power set of the power set of a $2$-element set.
Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between $A\times B$ and $B\times A$?
choices: They are always equal as sets.
They are always in bijection but equal only when $A=B$ or one of them is empty.
They are in bijection via $(a,b)\mapsto(b,a)$, but as sets $A\times B\ne B\times A$ in general (even when $|A|=|B|>0$ and $A\ne B$).
They are equal whenever $|A|=|B|$.
hard.explain: The swap map $(a,b)\mapsto(b,a)$ is a bijection $A\times B\to B\times A$, so they have the same cardinality. However, as sets of ordered pairs they differ: $(a,b)\in A\times B$ has first coordinate in $A$, while $(b,a)\in B\times A$ has first coordinate in $B$. If $A\cap B=\emptyset$ and both are non-empty, no element of $A\times B$ belongs to $B\times A$.

hard.q: How many injective functions are there from a $3$-element set to a $5$-element set?
hard.explain: The first element has $5$ choices, the second $4$, the third $3$: $5\cdot 4\cdot 3=60$.

questions.q: How many equivalence classes does the relation '$a \sim b$ iff $5 \mid a - b$' on $\mathbb{Z}$ have?
questions.hint: The classes are $[0], [1], [2], [3], [4]$ — the residue classes modulo $5$.
questions.explain: The classes are $[0], [1], [2], [3], [4]$ — the residue classes modulo $5$. So $|\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}| = 5$.

questions.q: Which of the following relations on $\mathbb{Z}$ is an equivalence relation?
choices: $a \sim b \iff a \le b$
$a \sim b \iff a \ne b$
$a \sim b \iff a^2 = b^2$
$a \sim b \iff a \mid b$
questions.hint: $a \sim b \iff a^2 = b^2$ is reflexive ($a^2 = a^2$), symmetric ($a^2 = b^2 \Rightarrow b^2 = a^2$), and transitive.
questions.explain: $a \sim b \iff a^2 = b^2$ is reflexive ($a^2 = a^2$), symmetric ($a^2 = b^2 \Rightarrow b^2 = a^2$), and transitive. The others fail: $\le$ is not symmetric, $\ne$ is not reflexive (and not transitive), and $\mid$ is not symmetric.

questions.q: Let $\pi\colon X \to X/{\sim}$ be the quotient map. Which statement correctly expresses the universal property of the quotient?
choices: Every function $X \to Y$ factors uniquely through $\pi$.
A function $f\colon X \to Y$ factors uniquely through $\pi$ iff $f$ is constant on $\sim$-classes.
A function $f\colon X \to Y$ factors uniquely through $\pi$ iff $f$ is injective.
A function $X/{\sim} \to Y$ factors through $\pi$ iff it is surjective.
questions.hint: The universal property: $f\colon X \to Y$ descends to a (unique) $\bar f\colon X/{\sim} \to Y$ with $\bar f \circ \pi = f$ exactly when $f$ respects the equivalence — i.e.
questions.explain: The universal property: $f\colon X \to Y$ descends to a (unique) $\bar f\colon X/{\sim} \to Y$ with $\bar f \circ \pi = f$ exactly when $f$ respects the equivalence — i.e. $x \sim x' \Rightarrow f(x) = f(x')$.

hard.q: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ is reflexive and transitive but fails symmetry. Which of the following is a correct counterexample?
choices: Divisibility $\mid$ on $\mathbb{N}_{>0}$: $2\mid 4$ but $4\nmid 2$, and $\mid$ is reflexive and transitive.
Equality $=$ on $\mathbb{Z}$: it is reflexive, transitive, and symmetric, so it is an equivalence relation.
The empty relation on a non-empty set: it is symmetric and transitive but not reflexive.
The relation $a\sim b\iff a^2\le b^2$ on $\mathbb{Z}$: it fails transitivity.
hard.explain: Divisibility on $\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ is a partial order (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive) but not symmetric: $2\mid 4$ yet $4\nmid 2$. It therefore shows that reflexivity + transitivity do not force symmetry.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the kernel equivalence $x\sim_f y\iff f(x)=f(y)$ yields the canonical factorisation $f = \bar f\circ\pi$?
choices: Define $\bar f([x])=f(x)$ → check well-definedness (same class gives same value) → verify $\bar f\circ\pi = f$ → show $\bar f$ is injective on $X/\sim_f$
Show $\bar f$ is surjective → define $\bar f$ → check transitivity of $\sim_f$
Prove $\pi$ is bijective → factor $f$ through $\pi^{-1}$
Check $f$ is surjective → define quotient → apply universal property
hard.explain: Standard proof: first define $\bar f([x])=f(x)$, then verify well-definedness ($x\sim_f y\Rightarrow f(x)=f(y)$), then observe $\bar f(\pi(x))=\bar f([x])=f(x)$. Injectivity of $\bar f$ follows because $\bar f([x])=\bar f([y])$ implies $f(x)=f(y)$ implies $[x]=[y]$.

hard.q: The relation $\sim$ on $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ is defined by $a\sim b\iff 3\mid a-b$. How many equivalence classes are there, and what is the size of the class $[2]$?  Enter the size of $[2]$.

questions.q: Which of the following sets is uncountable?
choices: $\mathbb{Q}$
$\mathbb{Z}^3$
the set of algebraic numbers
$\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$
questions.hint: By Cantor's theorem $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| > |\mathbb{N}|$, so $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ is uncountable (in fact $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| = 2^{\aleph_0} = \mathfrak{c} = |\mathbb{R}|$).
questions.explain: By Cantor's theorem $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| > |\mathbb{N}|$, so $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ is uncountable (in fact $|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})| = 2^{\aleph_0} = \mathfrak{c} = |\mathbb{R}|$). The other three are countable: $\mathbb{Q}$ injects into $\mathbb{Z}^2$; $\mathbb{Z}^3$ is a finite product of countables; the algebraic numbers are a countable union of finite root-sets of integer polynomials.

questions.q: Let $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ be an enumeration of binary sequences with $s_0 = 0110\dots$, $s_1 = 1001\dots$, $s_2 = 0011\dots$, $s_3 = 1111\dots$. What are the first four bits of the Cantor diagonal sequence $d$ defined by $d_n = 1 - s_n(n)$?
questions.explain: The diagonal entries are $s_0(0) = 0$, $s_1(1) = 0$, $s_2(2) = 1$, $s_3(3) = 1$. Flipping each bit gives $d = 1100\dots$, which differs from each $s_n$ in position $n$.

questions.q: Under the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i, j \rangle = \tfrac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2} + i$ (with $i, j \ge 0$), what is $\langle 2, 1 \rangle$?

hard.q: Cantor's theorem states $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for every set $A$. Which step in the diagonal proof is the critical one that produces a contradiction?
choices: Assuming $f\colon A\to\mathcal{P}(A)$ is injective and deriving a cardinality bound
Defining $D=\{a\in A\mid a\notin f(a)\}$ and noting $D\in\mathcal{P}(A)$, then showing $D\notin\operatorname{im} f$
Showing $\mathcal{P}(A)$ injects into $A$ via the power-set axiom
Applying the Schröder–Bernstein theorem to $A$ and $\mathcal{P}(A)$
hard.hint: Build a subset guaranteed to differ from every $f(a)$ on the element $a$ itself.
hard.explain: Assume for contradiction $f\colon A\to\mathcal{P}(A)$ is surjective. Form the diagonal set $D=\{a\in A\mid a\notin f(a)\}$. Since $f$ is surjective, $D=f(d)$ for some $d$. Then $d\in D\iff d\notin f(d)=D$, a contradiction. Hence no surjection $A\to\mathcal{P}(A)$ exists.

hard.q: Which is a correct proof-step ordering for showing $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable via the zigzag enumeration?
choices: Well-order $\mathbb{Q}$ → extract a bijection with $\mathbb{N}$ → conclude countability

Embed $\mathbb{Q}$ into $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ via $p/q\mapsto(p,q)$ in lowest terms → note $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ is countable as a product of two countable sets → conclude $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable
Show $|\mathbb{Q}|=|\mathbb{R}|$ and then argue $\mathbb{R}$ is countable
Apply Cantor's theorem to $\mathbb{Z}$ to get $|\mathbb{Q}|<|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{Z})|=\mathfrak{c}$
hard.explain: Writing each rational in lowest terms gives an injection $\mathbb{Q}\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$. A countable product of countable sets is countable (shown by the zigzag/Cantor pairing), so $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}_{>0}$ is countable, and any subset of a countable set is countable.

hard.q: Using the Cantor zigzag pairing $\langle i,j\rangle=\frac{(i+j)(i+j+1)}{2}+i$, compute $\langle 3,0\rangle$.

questions.q: Which of the following is equivalent to the axiom of choice over ZF?
choices: The continuum hypothesis: there is no cardinal strictly between $\aleph_0$ and $\mathfrak{c}$.
Zorn's lemma: every non-empty poset in which every chain has an upper bound has a maximal element.
Cantor's theorem: $|\mathcal{P}(A)| > |A|$ for every set $A$.
Russell's paradox: there is no set of all sets.
questions.hint: Zorn's lemma is one of the standard equivalents of AC, alongside the well-ordering theorem and Tukey's lemma.
questions.explain: Zorn's lemma is one of the standard equivalents of AC, alongside the well-ordering theorem and Tukey's lemma. The other choices are theorems of ZF or ZFC but are not equivalent to AC — the continuum hypothesis is independent of ZFC, and Cantor's theorem is provable in ZF with no choice at all.

questions.q: Russell's distinction between shoes and socks illustrates which point?
choices: For finite families of non-empty sets, AC is already provable from the other axioms of ZF.
For infinite families of non-empty sets, a uniform rule for picking one element may fail to exist even though AC asserts a choice function still does.
AC is false whenever the sets are indistinguishable.
The axiom of choice is equivalent to the well-ordering of $\mathbb{R}$ only for ordered pairs.
questions.hint: With shoes there is a uniform rule ('pick the left one'), so no AC is needed.
questions.explain: With shoes there is a uniform rule ('pick the left one'), so no AC is needed. With socks there is no such rule, yet AC still asserts the product $\prod_i A_i$ is non-empty. The point is that for infinite families we can always produce a choice function under AC, even when we cannot write down an explicit one.

questions.q: Which of these standard results is not known to require (some form of) the axiom of choice?
choices: Every vector space has a basis.
An arbitrary product of compact topological spaces is compact (Tychonoff).
Every commutative ring with $1\ne 0$ has a maximal ideal.
A countable union of countable sets is countable.
questions.hint: The first three all use full AC (or Zorn's lemma, which is equivalent).
questions.explain: The first three all use full AC (or Zorn's lemma, which is equivalent). The countable-union-of-countable-sets statement only needs the weaker countable choice, and over ZF plus countable choice it is provable — but in ZF alone it is not. The question asks for the statement that is 'not known to require AC' (i.e. full choice).

hard.q: Which is the correct logical chain showing AC $\Rightarrow$ Well-Ordering Theorem $\Rightarrow$ Zorn's Lemma $\Rightarrow$ AC (the classical equivalence circle)?
choices: AC gives a choice function on all non-empty subsets of any set, which is used to build a well-ordering by transfinite recursion; a well-ordering on a poset yields a maximal chain and hence a maximal element; from Zorn one constructs a choice function by taking maximal elements of finite sub-families.
AC is strictly stronger than the Well-Ordering Theorem, and Zorn's Lemma is independent of both.
Zorn's Lemma implies AC directly without passing through the Well-Ordering Theorem.
The three statements are equivalent only for sets of size at most $\aleph_1$.
hard.explain: All three are equivalent over ZF. AC → Well-Ordering: use the choice function to pick the least element at each transfinite stage. Well-Ordering → Zorn: in a well-ordered poset every chain has an upper bound (its supremum in the well-order), so a maximal element exists. Zorn → AC: apply Zorn to the poset of partial choice functions ordered by extension.

hard.q: In ZF without AC, which of the following can be proved?
choices: Every infinite set has a countably infinite subset.
The reals $\mathbb{R}$ can be well-ordered.
Cantor's theorem: $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$ for every set $A$.
Every surjection has a right inverse (section).
hard.explain: Cantor's diagonal theorem requires no choice whatsoever — the diagonal set $D=\{a\mid a\notin f(a)\}$ is defined explicitly. The existence of a countably infinite subset of every infinite set requires (at least) Dependent Choice; well-ordering $\mathbb{R}$ needs full AC; and right inverses for surjections are equivalent to AC.

hard.q: Vitali's construction of a non-measurable subset of $[0,1]$ uses AC in which essential step?
choices: Choosing one representative from each coset of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$, which requires selecting from uncountably many non-empty sets with no definable rule.
Proving $[0,1]$ is uncountable via Cantor diagonalisation.
Applying the triangle inequality to the Lebesgue outer measure.
Showing that a countable union of measure-zero sets has measure zero.
hard.explain: The Vitali set $V$ is built by picking exactly one element from each equivalence class of $x\sim y\iff x-y\in\mathbb{Q}$ restricted to $[0,1]$. There are uncountably many classes and no canonical choice; AC is invoked to guarantee a simultaneous selection. Assuming $V$ is measurable then leads to a contradiction with countable additivity.
